comments.text: (i) P. E. Haxell in [Ha01] has proved the conjecture for graphs that do not contain $K_{2,s}$, where $s= \lfloor{\frac{k}{18}}\rfloor$. (ii) S. Balasubramanian and E. Dobson in [BaDo07] has improved upon [Ha01] to include graphs that do not contain $K_{2,s}$, where $s< \frac{k}{12}+1$. (iii) The result of [WLL00] (discussed in the problem description) has been improved by Dobson in [Do02] to include graphs whose complements do not contain $K_{2,4}$. (iv) A. F. Sidorenko in [Si89] proved the conjecture for every tree with a vertex having atleast $\lceil {\frac{k}{2}}\rceil - 1$ leaf-neighbours. (v) This result has been improved by N. Eaton and G. Tiner in [EaTi10] to include every tree with a vertex having at least $\lceil {\frac{k}{2}}\rceil - 2$ leaf-neighbours. They have also showed that this conjecture holds for values of $k$ at most $8$. (vi) G. Tiner and Z. Tomlin in [TiTo22] has proved that the conjecture holds for $k=9$. (vii) B. Zhou in [Zh84] has proved the conjecture in the case when graph $G$ has a number of vertices $k$. (viii) P. Slater, S. Teo and H. Yap in [STY85] has proved the conjecture in the case when graph $G$ has a number of vertices $k+1$. (ix) M. Wozniak in [Wo96] has proved the conjecture for trees of diameter at most $4$ having at most one vertex with degree exceeding $2$. This paper proves the conjecture in the case when graph $G$ has a number of vertices $k+2$. (x) G. Tiner in [Ti10] has proved the conjecture in the case when graph $G$ has a number of vertices $k+3$. (xi) L. Yuan and X. Zhang in [YuZh17] has proved the conjecture in the case when graph $G$ has a number of vertices $k+4$. (xii) A spider is a tree with at most one vertex of degree more than $2$. G. Fan, Y. Hong and Q. Liu in [FHL18] has proved that the conjecture holds for all spiders. (xiii) Balasubramaniam in his PhD thesis [Ba] has proved the conjecture for graphs containing no $K_{2,s}$ where, $s ≥ 2$ and $k > 12(s − 1)$. [EaTi10]: N. Eaton, G. Tiner, On the Erdős-Sós Conjecture and Graphs with large minimum degree, Ars Combin. 95 (2010) 373–382. Plus it seems that a solution was announced for this conjecture by Ajtai, Komlós, Simonovits, and Szemerédi, but it's whereabouts are unknown...
